Set 1.text: Your teacher suddenly calls your name while you were daydreaming in class. You feel your heart jump. What do you do next?
options: A) Blame the teacher in your head for picking on you and sit down angrily.
B) Take a quick breath, realize you lost focus, and politely ask the teacher to repeat the question.
C) Make a silly joke to hide your embarrassment and get the class to laugh.
D) Stand completely frozen, stare at the floor, and wait for the teacher to move on.

Set 1.text: A classmate takes your favorite pen without asking and accidentally breaks it. How do you handle your anger?
options: A) Snatch something valuable belonging to them and break it right back to make things even.
B) Scream at them in front of the whole class so they feel as bad as you do.
C) Take a deep breath to cool down, then tell them firmly that they need to replace or fix it.
D) Say nothing but stay angry all day, complaining about them behind their back.

Set 1.text: You practiced hard for the school cricket/football selection match, but your name is not on the final list. What is your reaction?
options: A) Decide that sports are stupid and resolve never to participate in school selections again.
B) Assume the coach is biased and complain to everyone that the selection was unfair.
C) Feel disappointed but ask the coach what specific skills you need to practice to make the team next time.
D) Pretend you don't care at all and tell your friends you didn't really want to be on the team anyway.

Set 1.text: During lunch break, a classmate accidentally drops their tiffin box, and all their food spills onto the dirty floor. What do you do?
options: A) Laugh loudly with your friends because it looked funny.
B) Ignore it completely and carry on eating your own lunch with your back turned.
C) Walk over, ask if they are okay, and offer to share your own tiffin with them.
D) Tell them to clean it up quickly before the teacher arrives and gives them a scolding.

Set 1.text: You are chosen as the leader for a science group project, but one member is being lazy and doing absolutely nothing. How do you handle it?
options: A) Do all the work yourself but leave their name completely off the project cover sheet without telling them.
B) Talk to them privately to understand if they find the topic hard, and reassign a simpler task to them.
C) Yell at them during group work and call them lazy in front of the whole class.
D) Complain to the teacher immediately without speaking to the teammate first.

Set 2.text: Right before standing up to give a speech in the school morning assembly, you feel your stomach tighten and your palms sweat. What does this mean to you?
options: A) It means I am weak and not built for public speaking, so I should avoid it.
B) It is a sign that my body is naturally nervous, but it's a normal reaction that will pass once I start.
C) It means I am going to faint, so I should quickly ask to go to the medical room.
D) I try to pretend my body isn't doing anything and ignore the feeling completely.

Set 2.text: Your younger sibling accidentally runs into your room and breaks your school project model that took days to build. What is your response?
options: A) Immediately smash one of their favorite toys to teach them a lesson.
B) Lock yourself in your room, cry bitterly, and refuse to eat dinner.
C) Take a moment to calm down, tell them to be careful, and figure out how to repair it before school.
D) Scream loudly until your parents come and punish your sibling.

Set 2.text: You are trying to learn a difficult math formula or programming block and fail three times in a row. What do you tell yourself?
options: A) 'I'm just not smart enough for this subject, I should focus on easier things.'
B) 'The textbook is written terribly, and no one could understand this.'
C) 'I need to try a different method or ask an elder/teacher to explain it differently.'
D) 'I will just skip this question in the exam and focus on memorizing other parts.'

Set 2.text: A new student joins your class mid-term. During recess, they are sitting alone in a corner looking quietly at their shoes. What goes through your mind?
options: A) 'They probably want to be left alone, so I shouldn't bother them.'
B) 'They must feel really out of place and nervous. I should go introduce myself.'
C) 'If they want friends, they should come talk to my group instead of sitting there.'
D) 'They look a bit strange, I wonder why they changed schools mid-year.'

Set 2.text: Two of your closest friends have an argument and both demand that you stop talking to the other person. How do you handle it?
options: A) Pick the friend who is more popular so you don't lose social standing.
B) Stop talking to both of them because you don't want to deal with their drama.
C) Tell them you value both of them individually and refuse to take sides, but offer to listen to both.
D) Secretly hang out with one and tell the other person bad things about them to keep both happy.

Set 3.text: On a very hot afternoon, you find yourself snapping at your parents and feeling highly irritated over tiny things. How do you reflect on this?
options: A) Realize that your physical discomfort (heat, fatigue) is leaking into your mood, and take a cold drink.
B) Assume your parents are intentionally trying to annoy you today.
C) Think that you are naturally becoming an angry person and feel bad about your personality.
D) Don't think about it at all; just stay in a bad mood until it goes away naturally.

Set 3.text: The class teacher harshly accuses you of talking loudly, but it was actually the person sitting right behind you. How do you respond?
options: A) Stand up and shout back, 'It wasn't me! You always blame me unfairly!'
B) Keep quiet, glare at the teacher with an angry face, and refuse to do any classwork.
C) Calmly state that it wasn't you, but accept the instruction to stay quiet, and speak to the teacher after class.
D) Point your finger immediately at the student behind you and say, 'He did it, punish him!'

Set 3.text: You receive your marks for a subject you usually top in, and you find you scored a very low C grade. What is your primary reaction?
options: A) Hide the mark sheet from your parents and convince yourself the teacher made a huge grading error.
B) Feel completely devastated, conclude you're losing your intelligence, and cry.
C) Review the paper objectively to find your weak spots, and plan a better study strategy for the finals.
D) Say, 'At least I passed, it doesn't matter much anyway,' and ignore the result.

Set 3.text: Your close friend's pet dog passes away. They come to school but sit very quietly without talking. What is your approach?
options: A) Try to crack jokes and make them laugh to distract them from thinking about it.
B) Sit quietly next to them, let them know you are sorry for their loss, and just listen if they want to speak.
C) Tell them, 'It's just an animal, you can always buy a new one, don't be sad.'
D) Give them space by not talking to them or looking at them all day.

Set 3.text: During a games period, you want to play basketball but your group of friends insists on playing football. What do you do?
options: A) Refuse to play anything, sit on the bench alone, and make your friends feel guilty.
B) Propose a compromise: 'Let's play football today, and basketball during the next recess/period!'
C) Forcefully argue until some people get annoyed and give in to what you want.
D) Silently join the football game but play badly on purpose because you are unhappy.

Set 4.text: Your friend arrives at school with a brand-new, expensive sports bicycle. You feel a heavy, uncomfortable feeling inside. How do you process this?
options: A) Acknowledge to yourself that you are feeling jealous, and remind yourself that it's okay to want nice things without resenting your friend.
B) Tell yourself that your friend is showing off and doesn't deserve such a nice bicycle.
C) Ignore the feeling and look for flaws in their bicycle to make yourself feel better.
D) Assume that your parents don't love you enough because they haven't bought you a similar bicycle.

Set 4.text: You are playing an online or casual multi-player game with friends, and your character keeps losing because of poor internet connection. What do you do?
options: A) Throw the device down or slam the keyboard in a rage.
B) Start typing angry words in the game chat, blaming your teammates or the game makers.
C) Recognize your growing frustration, step away from the game, and do something offline to cool down.
D) Keep playing while getting more and more miserable and complaining loudly to your family.

Set 4.text: A massive unexpected downpour cancels a long-awaited school field trip. What is your internal response?
options: A) Complain that everything good always gets ruined and ruin the rest of your day at home.
B) Accept that the weather is out of anyone's control, and organize a fun indoor game or watch a movie instead.
C) Get angry at the school management for not checking the weather reports better.
D) Go to sleep for the rest of the day out of pure frustration and boredom.

Set 4.text: You see a student from a lower class being openly teased and mocked by a few seniors near the school staircase. What is your direct reaction?
options: A) Join in the laughing so the seniors think you are cool and mature.
B) Walk away quickly because you don't want the seniors to target you next.
C) Inform a nearby teacher immediately or stand by the student to help them walk away from the situation safely.
D) Stay and watch the drama like it is an interesting street movie.

Set 4.text: During a family function, your cousin takes full credit for a creative party game that you actually came up with. How do you handle it?
options: A) Expose them loudly in front of all the relatives, calling them a liar and a thief.
B) Say nothing at the time, but talk to them privately later and say, 'Hey, it hurt me when you didn't mention we did that together. Let's share credit next time.'
C) Never speak to that cousin again and tell your parents how horrible they are.
D) Just smile and let them have all the credit, but feel deeply bitter inside.

Set 5.text: You have coaching classes, school homework, and a project all due tomorrow. You feel paralyzed and can't start. What are you aware of?
options: A) I realize that feeling overwhelmed is making me stuck, so I need to break the work into tiny, bite-sized tasks.
B) I decide that I am lazy and don't have good time-management skills.
C) I conclude that my teachers are malicious and are intentionally plotting to ruin my evening.
D) I ignore the anxiety and watch videos on my phone to make the bad feeling disappear.

Set 5.text: Your parent comes home stressed from the office and scolds you for a minor messy spot in your room before you can explain. What do you do?
options: A) Slam your room door shut and shout, 'You never understand anything!'
B) Wait for them to settle down and change, then calmly say, 'I was just finishing my project, I will clean it up now.'
C) Start crying loudly and tell them they love your sibling more than you.
D) Clean the room aggressively while throwing items around to show your frustration.

Set 5.text: You enter a school debate or quiz competition, but your team gets knocked out in the very first round. How do you handle it?
options: A) Tell your teammates they did a horrible job and it's their fault you lost.
B) Decide that competition isn't your thing and pledge never to register again.
C) Congratulate the winning team, and think about what points they made that you can learn from.
D) Shrug it off and say the judges were biased anyway, so it doesn't matter.

Set 5.text: Your school teacher looks visibly exhausted, with a hoarse voice, trying hard to control a loud, unruly class. What is your reaction?
options: A) Take advantage of the chaos to talk loudly with your desk partner since the teacher can't catch everyone.
B) Sit quietly, follow instructions immediately, and politely ask your immediate neighbors to tone down their noise.
C) Stare out of the window and wait for the period bell to ring, ignoring the noise.
D) Laugh at how the teacher has completely lost control of the classroom.

Set 5.text: A friend you hang out with every single day suddenly stops sitting next to you or talking to you, without giving a reason. What do you do?
options: A) Start spreading nasty rumors about them to other classmates before they can do it to you.
B) Approach them privately and ask calmly, 'Hey, I noticed you've been a bit distant. Did I do something to upset you?'
C) Pretend they don't exist and completely ignore them for the rest of the year.
D) Send them an angry text message demanding to know what their problem is.

Set 6.text: You see group photos on a classmate's social media or WhatsApp status showing your close friends hanging out together without inviting you. What do you notice?
options: A) I feel a deep pang of rejection and envy, and I need to process this hurt before reacting to them.
B) I realize that none of my friends ever liked me, and I have always been completely alone.
C) I feel nothing; I don't care about what others do outside of school hours.
D) I get filled with rage and start planning how to create a new group to exclude them.

Set 6.text: During a tough terminal math exam, you notice that your friend's answer sheet is easily visible from your seat. You are stuck on a 5-mark question. What do you do?
options: A) Look carefully and copy their steps; it's okay because everyone cheats sometimes.
B) Take a breath, look away, accept that your marks should reflect your own preparation, and try your best on your own sheet.
C) Glance just to check the final answer number so you can manipulate your own working to match it.
D) Stare at their paper for a long time, feeling deeply conflicted and wasting precious exam time.

Set 6.text: Your science exhibition model completely stops working right as the judges enter your aisle. What is your action?
options: A) Burst into tears, cover your face, and let your project partner do all the talking.
B) Explain the underlying theory confidently to the judges, explain what part broke down technically, and discuss how you would fix it.
C) Kick the table or model in frustration and walk out of the exhibition room.
D) Pretend that the model was never supposed to move and tell a flat lie to the judges.

Set 6.text: A nervous classmate starts stuttering and mixing up words while presenting an individual speech in front of the whole class. What do you do?
options: A) Snicker quietly behind your hand and whisper something funny to your desk partner.
B) Maintain encouraging eye contact, nod along, and clap warmly when they finish the presentation.
C) Stare at your desk, feeling incredibly awkward, wishing the period would end soon.
D) Shout out a correction from your seat to 'help' them with the word they are stuck on.

Set 6.text: You really want to go on a 3-day school educational tour, but your parents are hesitant due to safety and cost concerns. How do you talk to them?
options: A) Throw a massive tantrum, lock yourself in your room, and threaten to skip your upcoming exams.
B) Sit down with them, show them the detailed itinerary, share the teacher's phone number, and offer to save pocket money to help with costs.
C) Constantly whine and nag them every hour until they finally agree just to stop your nagging.
D) Secretly forge their signature on the permission slip and figure out the money details later.

Set 7.text: You have to choose one hobby club (Drama, Coding, Painting, or Debate). How do you make your choice?
options: A) Join whatever club my best friend joins, because I can't survive a class without them.
B) Reflect on what activities actually match my personal strengths and interests, even if I don't know anyone else in that club.
C) Pick the club that is rumored to have the least amount of actual work or homework.
D) Let my parents pick the club for me because they know better than me anyway.

Set 7.text: Someone running fast down a crowded school corridor collides with you heavily, causing you to drop all your open notebook sheets into a puddle. What is your reaction?
options: A) Hurl an insult at them immediately and push them back with force.
B) Take a deep breath, accept that it was an accident in a crowded space, and accept their apology while gathering the sheets.
C) Sit down on the floor and start crying over your ruined notes.
D) Scream at the top of your lungs for a corridor monitor or physical education teacher to catch them.

Set 7.text: You spend the whole weekend painting a landscape for a class chart display, but when hung up, it looks much less skilled than others. What do you feel?
options: A) Rip your painting down when no one is looking and throw it in the dustbin.
B) Recognize that art takes time to build up, appreciate the techniques used by others, and ask a talented classmate for tips.
C) Tell everyone that painting is a waste of time and that you only care about sports anyway.
D) Assume the teacher gave the best display spots to their favorite students.

Set 7.text: A student in your class has been distracted, missing homework submissions because their mother is hospitalized. How do you interact with them?
options: A) Remind them during group work that they are dragging down the group's overall internal marks.
B) Offer to share your neat class notes and help them catch up with lessons during free periods.
C) Avoid them because talking about hospitals and sickness makes you feel deeply uncomfortable.
D) Ask them for all the dramatic details of the sickness so you can share the update with others.

Set 7.text: A friend mistakenly hears a rumor that you spoke poorly of them behind their back, and they confront you angrily. What is your action?
options: A) Get defensive and say, 'If you believe rumors, you were never a real friend anyway!' and walk away.
B) Listen fully to what they heard, remain calm, clarify the truth honestly, and show them you respect the friendship.
C) Counter-attack by listing all the mistakes they have made in the past to deflect the focus.
D) Apologize profusely for something you didn't do just to make the argument stop immediately.

Set 8.text: You keep putting off opening your history textbook for days, preferring to play games, even though the exam is near. What do you realize?
options: A) I realize I am delaying because I find history confusing and am scared of failing, so I need to ask for help.
B) I realize I am just a lazy student who is fundamentally incapable of working hard.
C) I tell myself that history is a useless subject, so it doesn't matter if I study it later.
D) I don't realize anything; I just keep playing games and try not to think about the book.

Set 8.text: The household internet cuts out entirely right as you are about to click 'Submit' on a timed holiday homework assignment form. How do you manage your panic?
options: A) Scream, throw your mouse, and blame your family members for using up the data.
B) Take a slow breath, use a mobile hotspot if available, or write a polite email/SMS to the teacher explaining the issue immediately.
C) Abandon the submission entirely, lie down, and decide to accept a zero score without attempting a fix.
D) Keep frantically clicking the broken button over and over while crying hysterically.

Set 8.text: The teacher introduces a brand new, highly complex math chapter. After two explanations, you still don't understand a single step. What do you do?
options: A) Assume you're just bad at advanced math and mentally tune out for the rest of the term.
B) Mark the difficult steps with a pencil, check online video tutorials at home, or ask a peer for a peer-to-peer explanation.
C) Complain loudly to your friends that the teacher has no idea how to teach properly.
D) Copy the solved examples blindly into your notebook without bothering to understand them.

Set 8.text: You see the school support staff (security guard or cleaning auntie) working hard in the scorching 42-degree summer heat without a fan nearby. What is your thought?
options: A) Think that it is their job and they are paid for it, so there is nothing unusual about it.
B) Feel a sense of respect and care, and offer them a cold bottle of water from the water cooler if you can access one.
C) Avoid looking at them because seeing people struggle makes you feel uncomfortable.
D) Wonder why they didn't study harder when they were young so they could have an AC office.

Set 8.text: There is a massive disagreement in your sports team about who should be the opening batsman or team captain. How do you resolve it?
options: A) Demand that you be the leader, otherwise you will take your sports kit and go home.
B) Suggest a fair voting system or an objective trial match, and agree beforehand to support whoever wins the selection.
C) Form a mini-secret clique within the team to talk bad about the other candidate until they step down.
D) Step away entirely and let the most aggressive person have the spot just to keep things quiet.

Set 9.text: At the start of the new academic year, you are placed in a different section away from all your old friends. You feel empty inside. How do you read this?
options: A) Identify that you are experiencing transition anxiety and loneliness, which is natural, and give yourself a few weeks to adjust.
B) Tell yourself that the school authorities hate you and deliberately separated your group.
C) Assume you will never make a single friend ever again and feel miserable.
D) Force yourself to act overly loud and aggressive in the new class to hide your inner fear.

Set 9.text: A classmate shares a juicy, embarrassing rumor about another student's family life with you during a break. What is your direct response?
options: A) Immediately pass it on to three other friends via a private WhatsApp message because it's interesting.
B) Listen, but tell them firmly that you don't care about rumors, and choose not to repeat it to anyone.
C) Ask for more specific details so you can know the full story perfectly.
D) Nod along silently, but feel intensely uncomfortable and judgmental inside without saying anything.

Set 9.text: Your favorite English or Science teacher who inspired you suddenly leaves the school mid-session. How do you approach the new teacher?
options: A) Refuse to pay attention in the new teacher's class, constantly comparing them poorly to the old one.
B) Welcome the new teacher politely, stay open-minded, and maintain your excellent study habits regardless of who teaches.
C) Complain to your parents that your education is completely ruined because of the school changes.
D) Do only the bare minimum work needed to pass, since the inspiration is gone.

Set 9.text: A classmate stands up to answer a teacher's question, gives a completely incorrect answer, and the entire back bench bursts into loud laughs. How do you act?
options: A) Join the laugh because the answer sounded genuinely ridiculous.
B) Do not laugh, look at them with a reassuring smile, and perhaps raise your hand to offer a constructive perspective if needed.
C) Look out the door and pretend you didn't notice the classmate's embarrassment.
D) Whisper to your friend that the student should have prepared better before opening their mouth.

Set 9.text: Your team wants to organize a small farewell surprise for a departing monitor, but half the group wants a card and half wants a cake. It's causing a deadlock. What do you do?
options: A) Split the team into two warring factions and tell everyone to do their own separate thing.
B) Suggest combining them: a small budget handmade card alongside a basic shared cake, keeping everyone's inputs valid.
C) Scream at the group that they are wasting time over small things and leave the committee.
D) Sit back and let them argue until the deadline passes and nothing gets done.

Set 10.text: You feel completely bored and sleepy during a long history or geography chapter reading. How do you analyze this state?
options: A) Conclude that the subject is garbage and you are simply not cut out for reading text heavy books.
B) Recognize that your brain needs active engagement, so you decide to make quick sketches or highlight key terms to stay sharp.
C) Blame the teacher's monotonous voice completely for your lack of focus.
D) Force yourself to stare blankly at the page while your mind travels to other imaginary worlds.

Set 10.text: Your crucial final exams are starting next Monday, but your favorite gaming stream or show goes live every evening this week. How do you regulate yourself?
options: A) Watch the live stream for hours every evening and tell yourself you will pull an all-nighter right before the exam.
B) Set a firm limit: record or skip the streams during weekdays, study for 2 hours, and watch a 15-minute summary as a reward.
C) Delete all gaming apps in a panic, feel intensely miserable and irritated, and find yourself unable to study due to frustration.
D) Ask your parents to lock up your phone completely because you know you have no self-control.

Set 10.text: Your sports team loses a inter-house final match in the very last second because a teammate dropped an easy catch or missed a goal. What do you do?
options: A) Walk up to that specific teammate and loudly blame them for destroying the whole team's efforts.
B) Put an arm around their shoulder, say, 'Win or lose, we are a team. We fought well together,' and move on with grace.
C) Refuse to accept the runner-up medal and skip the closing ceremony photos.
D) Sit on the grass and sulk for an hour, refusing to talk to anyone in your family.

Set 10.text: Your usually funny and talkative desk partner comes to school with dark circles under their eyes and snaps aggressively at anyone who talks to them. What do you infer?
options: A) Think that they are becoming arrogant or mean, and decide to give them an angry retort back.
B) Realize that their sudden anger is likely a shield for deep exhaustion or trouble at home, and give them gentle, quiet space.
C) Complain to the class monitor that your partner is behaving badly today.
D) Ignore them completely and chat with friends on the row ahead.

Set 10.text: Your friends are planning a playground team game and explicitly say, 'Let's not take [Unpopular Student] because they are slow.' How do you step in?
options: A) Agree with your friends immediately because winning the game is the most important thing.
B) Say, 'Let's include everyone; we can rotate players or adapt the roles so it's fair and fun for everyone.'
C) Say nothing because you don't want your friends to think you are uncool or pushy.
D) Leave the game yourself in silent protest without explaining your reason to anyone.

Set 1.text: You realise you've read the same paragraph several times without understanding it. What do you do?
options: Blame the book and switch to your phone.
Realise you're tired or distracted, take a short break, and return later.
Keep reading without changing anything.
Decide you're just bad at concentrating.

Set 1.text: Your internet disconnects during an online class just before the teacher asks you a question. What do you do?
options: Keep blaming the internet provider.
Feel relieved that you missed the question.
Explain the problem to the teacher once you're back online and prepare for the missed question.
Panic and message everyone for help.

Set 1.text: You weren't selected for a school committee you really wanted to join. What do you do next?
options: Pretend you never wanted it.
Decide the selection was unfair and stop applying.
Believe you're not good enough for leadership roles.
Ask for feedback and prepare better for the next opportunity.

Set 1.text: A usually cheerful classmate has become quiet over the past few days. What do you do?
options: Wait for someone else to help.
Assume they just want to be left alone.
Ignore the change because it's not your concern.
Check in with them privately to see if they're okay.

Set 1.text: A misunderstanding creates tension between you and a friend. What do you do?
options: Avoid them for a few days.
Talk openly and clear up the misunderstanding.
End the friendship without discussing it.
Wait for them to apologise first.

Set 1.text: You've been feeling tired and unmotivated all week. What do you do first?
options: Blame everyone around you.
Ignore it and hope it gets better.
Assume you'll always feel this way.
Think about what's causing it before reacting.

Set 1.text: A parent checks your phone without asking and misunderstands an old message. How do you respond?
options: Get angry and shout.
Calmly explain the situation, then discuss how the privacy issue made you feel.
Delete your chats out of frustration.
Stay silent and avoid talking for days.

Set 1.text: A difficult chapter takes longer than expected to understand. What do you do?
options: Decide you're not smart enough.
Stop studying for the day without a plan.
Break it into smaller parts and keep learning.
Skip it and hope it isn't important.

Set 1.text: A new student is sitting alone during lunch. What do you do?
options: Invite them to join you or start a friendly conversation.
Assume they prefer being alone.
Ignore them because you don't know them.
Wait for someone else to include them.

Set 1.text: Your friends choose a different movie than the one you wanted. What do you do?
options: Agree but complain throughout the movie.
Watch the group's choice this time and suggest yours next time.
Skip the outing completely.
Keep insisting on your choice.

Set 2.text: You've been getting irritated with your friends all week. What do you do first?
options: Think about whether stress, lack of sleep, or exams are affecting your mood.
Decide you're just a bad friend.
Ignore it because it's probably nothing.
Assume your friends are the problem.

Set 2.text: You're arguing with your sibling over the family computer, and the argument is getting worse. What do you do?
options: Suggest taking a short break before talking again calmly.
Walk away and stop talking to them for the rest of the day.
Grab the computer from them.
Raise your voice to win the argument.

Set 2.text: Your proposal for a school event is rejected with very little feedback. What do you do?
options: Complain to your friends but take no further action.
Submit the same proposal again without changes.
Ask for feedback and improve it for the next opportunity.
Decide people don't like your ideas and stop trying.

Set 2.text: A friend seems happy but suddenly becomes quiet during lunch. What do you do?
options: Assume nothing is wrong.
Ignore the change.
Check in with them privately later.
Wait for someone else to ask.

Set 2.text: Two teammates disagree on how to complete a project. What do you do?
options: Let the disagreement continue.
Support one person without hearing the other side.
Stay out of the discussion completely.
Help them discuss both ideas and find a solution.

Set 2.text: You notice you're feeling nervous before trying something new. What do you do?
options: Pretend you're completely confident.
Accept the feeling and give yourself a chance to learn.
Decide not to try at all.
Tell yourself you'll probably fail.

Set 2.text: You receive disappointing news just before an important class. What do you do?
options: Stop paying attention completely.
Manage your emotions and focus on the class first.
Leave without telling anyone.
Let your emotions take over for the rest of the day.

Set 2.text: You've been learning guitar or coding for a month, but progress feels slow. What do you do?
options: Blame the tutorials for your slow progress.
Keep practising but keep complaining that nothing is working.
Decide you're not talented enough and quit.
Find out how long it usually takes to improve and adjust your practice plan.

Set 2.text: A friend smiles in class but seems unusually quiet afterward. What do you do?
options: Ask privately if everything is okay.
Assume they're just tired.
Ignore it because it's probably nothing.
Wait for them to start the conversation.

Set 2.text: Your team can't agree on an idea for a project. What do you do?
options: Push your own idea until others agree.
Let others decide without sharing your thoughts.
Stop participating because it's taking too long.
Help everyone compare ideas and find a solution together.

Set 3.text: You notice you've been avoiding one particular subject for several days. What do you do?
options: Blame the teacher without thinking further.
Tell yourself you're simply not good at it.
Keep avoiding it and hope it becomes easier later.
Think about why you're avoiding it and identify the reason.

Set 3.text: A group chat starts making fun of one of your friends, and everyone expects you to join in. What do you do?
options: Join in because everyone else is doing it.
Leave the chat without saying anything.
Privately message your friend to check if they're okay.
Decide it's not right and say you don't want to take part.

Set 3.text: You've made the same mistake more than once. What do you do?
options: Stop trying altogether.
Identify the reason and try a different approach.
Decide improvement isn't possible.
Keep repeating the same method.

Set 3.text: A classmate seems stressed before an exam. What do you do?
options: Offer encouragement or ask if they need help.
Tell them everyone feels stressed.
Make a joke about the situation.
Focus only on your own preparation.

Set 3.text: Two friends in your group are indirectly arguing through messages. What do you do?
options: Encourage them to talk directly and resolve the issue.
Leave the group chat quietly.
Complain about them to someone else.
Join in because it's entertaining.

Set 3.text: You see a post from a former close friend and feel a mix of emotions. What do you do?
options: Accept that you're feeling both hurt and nostalgic before reacting.
Feel angry that they're doing well.
Tell yourself you don't care at all.
Convince yourself the friendship never mattered.

Set 3.text: You receive an unexpected negative comment online. What do you do?
options: Keep rereading the comment and feel upset.
Reply immediately with an angry message.
Wait until you're calm before deciding how to respond.
Post something negative in return.

Set 3.text: You receive a lower grade than expected on an assignment. What do you do?
options: Stop putting in extra effort.
Decide the teacher doesn't like your work.
Review the feedback and improve for the next assignment.
Ignore the feedback and move on.

Set 3.text: A friend cancels plans with you for the third time this month with a vague excuse. What's your first thought?
options: I'll stop asking them to meet.
They may be dealing with something—I should check on them.
I'll do the same to them next time.
They're avoiding me on purpose.

Set 3.text: As class representative, you need to talk to a strict teacher about too much homework. What do you do?
options: Complain loudly during class.
Collect signatures before speaking to the teacher.
Say nothing and let everyone keep complaining.
Request a private meeting, share examples, and suggest a solution.

Set 4.text: You feel nervous before giving a presentation. How do you respond?
options: Accept that you're nervous and focus on doing your best.
Think you'll definitely fail.
Pretend you're not nervous at all.
Try not to think about it and hope it goes away.

Set 4.text: Someone posts an unfair comment about you on social media. What do you do?
options: Reply immediately with an angry message.
Keep checking the comments and feel upset all day.
Pause before responding and decide the best way to handle it.
Post something rude about them in return.

Set 4.text: You've been practising for a competition but aren't improving as quickly as expected. What do you do?
options: Change your practice strategy and keep working.
Decide you don't have enough talent.
Quit because progress is too slow.
Continue without changing anything and hope for better results.

Set 4.text: A younger student asks for advice about choosing a stream after Class 10. What do you do?
options: Send them to someone else without talking.
Ask about their interests before giving advice.
Give a quick answer because it isn't your responsibility.
Tell them to choose the stream with the best jobs.

Set 4.text: Your group has different opinions about a project deadline. What do you do?
options: Ignore the discussion.
Let others decide without participating.
Insist that your opinion is correct.
Help the group agree on a practical plan.

Set 4.text: After receiving feedback, you feel disappointed. What do you do first?
options: Believe you'll never improve.
Accept your feelings and think about how to improve.
Ignore the feedback completely.
Tell yourself the feedback doesn't matter.

Set 4.text: A teammate criticises your idea during a project discussion. What do you do?
options: Interrupt and defend yourself immediately.
Stay quiet but feel upset for the rest of the day.
Listen carefully and ask what could make the idea better.
Stop contributing to the discussion.

Set 4.text: You don't achieve a goal you worked hard for. What do you do next?
options: Reflect on what you learned and set a new goal.
Decide it isn't worth trying again.
Blame the situation without reviewing your effort.
Avoid similar challenges in the future.

Set 4.text: A classmate who usually scores well performs badly in a test and is trying not to cry. What do you do?
options: Tell them it's only one test.
Ask loudly what went wrong.
Give them space and don't mention it.
Quietly ask if they'd like to step outside or talk.

Set 4.text: You and your project partner strongly disagree, and the deadline is close. What do you do?
options: Go ahead with your idea because you're sure it's right.
Give in just to avoid conflict.
Present both ideas to the group and decide together.
Stop replying until they accept your idea.

Set 5.text: You realise you're feeling frustrated during homework. What do you do?
options: Keep working while feeling increasingly upset.
Tell yourself you're incapable of doing it.
Give up immediately.
Pause, understand what's causing it, and continue when ready.

Set 5.text: Someone says something unfair about you in front of others. What do you do?
options: Say something hurtful in return.
React immediately with anger.
Stay calm and respond respectfully.
Stay silent but hold a grudge.

Set 5.text: You didn't qualify for the finals of a competition you worked hard for. What do you do next?
options: Decide competitions aren't worth the effort.
Blame bad luck and move on.
Give up on similar opportunities.
Ask for feedback and prepare better for next time.

Set 5.text: A new student struggles to join conversations during group work. What do you do?
options: Let others include them instead.
Assume they're shy and leave them alone.
Invite them to share their ideas.
Ignore the situation.

Set 5.text: You and a friend have different opinions during a discussion. What do you do?
options: Listen to their point of view and share yours respectfully.
End the conversation without listening.
Keep arguing until they agree.
Avoid talking to them afterward.

Set 5.text: Just before an important test starts, you notice your leg shaking and your mind going blank. What do you think is happening?
options: I start worrying that I won't perform as well as I hoped.
I ignore it and hope it disappears once the test begins.
I tell myself the test doesn't really matter.
I realise it's normal test anxiety, take a deep breath, and remind myself I've prepared.

Set 5.text: A classmate unfairly blames you for a mistake during a group project. What do you do?
options: Argue loudly to prove you're right.
Stay calm and explain what actually happened.
Stay silent even though you're upset.
Get back at them later.

Set 5.text: You've been getting average marks in a subject you enjoy, even after working hard. What do you do?
options: Review your mistakes and change your study plan.
Assume you're just not good at the subject.
Blame the exam pattern.
Keep studying the same way and hope things improve.

Set 5.text: A friend often makes jokes about their own appearance. What do you think?
options: Change the topic because it feels awkward.
Tell them to stop being so negative.
Think they may be hiding an insecurity and check on them later.
Laugh along because it's just a joke.

Set 5.text: Your team finishes a successful project together. What do you do?
options: Focus only on your own role.
Appreciate everyone's contribution and celebrate together.
Say nothing and move on.
Point out what others could have done better first.

Set 1.text: Two weeks before your entrance exam, you notice you're either overworking or completely avoiding study, with no in-between. What's your read?
options: A) Recognise this swing pattern usually means underlying anxiety, and build a more sustainable daily structure.
B) Blame the exam system for being unreasonably stressful.
C) Push through the overworking phase since more hours must mean better results.
D) Decide you have no self-discipline and never will.

Set 1.text: A close friend gets a college offer you were hoping for yourself. You feel a sharp, uncomfortable reaction. What do you do?
options: A) Make an offhand comment implying their offer wasn't that impressive.
B) Notice the envy, let yourself feel it privately, and still congratulate them genuinely.
C) Avoid the topic completely whenever it comes up around them.
D) Distance yourself from them without explanation until the feeling passes.

Set 1.text: You score well below your target in a mock test series with the real exam a month away. What's your approach?
options: A) Decide mock tests don't reflect real exam performance anyway.
B) Conclude your target college is now out of reach and lower your ambitions immediately.
C) Break down the mock test by section to identify exactly where you're losing marks, and adjust your last month's plan.
D) Take another mock test the very next day without changing anything.

Set 1.text: A classmate who's usually competitive with you seems visibly deflated after a bad result. What's your move?
options: A) Feel a quiet sense of relief since you're ahead of them now.
B) Say nothing, since your relationship has always been about competition, not support.
C) Offer generic encouragement without really checking in on how they're feeling.
D) Set the rivalry aside for a moment and ask if they're doing okay.

Set 1.text: As student council member, you need to push back on an unpopular decision made by the principal's office, while representing student concerns. How do you approach it?
options: A) Request a meeting, come with organised student feedback, and propose a specific alternative.
B) Rally students to protest loudly before attempting any direct conversation.
C) Post about the unfairness on social media without addressing it internally first.
D) Avoid raising it since challenging authority feels too risky.

Set 2.text: You find yourself checking your phone every few minutes during a study session, even though you know it's hurting your focus. What's the honest read?
options: A) Tell yourself it's fine since you're 'multitasking'.
B) Recognise it's a dopamine-seeking habit under stress, and try a structured focus/break system instead.
C) Feel vaguely guilty about it but keep the phone within reach anyway.
D) Decide you simply don't have the willpower to be a serious student.

Set 2.text: A teacher dismisses a genuine doubt you raised in class as 'obvious', and a few classmates snicker. What's your in-the-moment response?
options: A) Stop asking questions in that class for the rest of the term.
B) Snap back defensively in front of the class.
C) Let it go in the moment, and if the doubt is still unresolved, ask the teacher privately after class.
D) Complain loudly to classmates about the teacher afterward.

Set 2.text: Your application essay for a college program gets rejected by a mentor's harsh first read. What's your next move?
options: A) Submit it as-is anyway since you're tired of revising.
B) Assume the mentor just has different taste and ignore the feedback.
C) Decide you're a bad writer and ask someone else to write it for you.
D) Ask for specific line-by-line feedback and revise it methodically over several drafts.

Set 2.text: A junior nervously approaches you for advice on handling a difficult teacher, clearly worried about repercussions. What's your approach?
options: A) Take their concern seriously, ask follow-up questions, and share what's actually worked for you.
B) Tell them to just deal with it since everyone has difficult teachers.
C) Give a quick, generic answer to move the conversation along.
D) Brush it off since it's not really your problem to solve.

Set 2.text: Your project group is relying heavily on one member who keeps missing deadlines, putting the whole grade at risk close to submission. How do you handle it?
options: A) Quietly redo their part yourself without saying anything to them.
B) Have a direct but respectful conversation about the risk, and agree on a concrete revised plan together.
C) Vent about them to the rest of the group without addressing it with the person directly.
D) Report them to the teacher immediately without talking to them first.

Set 3.text: You notice you're more irritable in weeks when you've been skipping meals or sleep due to studying. What's your takeaway?
options: A) Push through the exhaustion since results matter more right now.
B) Blame your family for not understanding how stressed you are.
C) Connect the irritability to specific physical habits, and prioritise sleep/meals even during intense study periods.
D) Decide you're just naturally short-tempered under academic stress.

Set 3.text: You're waitlisted for a college you really wanted, and the news comes right before a family gathering where relatives will ask about it. What do you do?
options: A) Avoid the gathering entirely to dodge the questions.
B) Snap irritably at the first relative who brings it up.
C) Lie about the outcome to avoid an uncomfortable conversation.
D) Prepare a calm, brief response in advance so you're not caught off guard by the questions.

Set 3.text: You've applied to five colleges and received two rejections in the same week. What's your mindset going into the remaining decisions?
options: A) Remind yourself that admissions decisions involve factors outside your control, and stay focused on what you can still influence.
B) Decide the whole college admissions process is fundamentally broken and unfair.
C) Assume the rejections predict how the rest will go and mentally prepare for failure.
D) Stop checking your application statuses altogether to avoid more disappointment.

Set 3.text: A close friend confides they're under intense pressure from their parents about career choice, and it's affecting their mental health. What's your response?
options: A) Change the subject since it feels like a heavy conversation to have.
B) Listen without immediately jumping to advice, and ask what kind of support would actually help them.
C) Tell them to just talk to their parents and it'll probably resolve itself.
D) Share your own similar (or different) experience at length instead of focusing on theirs.

Set 3.text: You're chosen to lead a fundraiser for a school cause, but two senior members openly resent taking instructions from you. How do you handle it?
options: A) Avoid giving them any responsibilities to sidestep the friction.
B) Assert your authority forcefully to make sure they follow your lead.
C) Have an early one-on-one with each of them to understand their concerns and find ways to use their strengths.
D) Complain to the teacher-in-charge about their attitude without addressing it directly first.

Set 4.text: You catch yourself assuming the worst (failing, embarrassment) before every important exam, even ones you're usually good at. What's the pattern?
options: A) Avoid thinking about the exam at all until the last minute.
B) Decide you're just a pessimist and there's nothing you can do about it.
C) Try to suppress the thought entirely without addressing why it keeps coming up.
D) Notice this as a recurring anxious thought pattern, and try reality-checking it against your actual track record.

Set 4.text: A friend group chat is planning something during a time you'd already committed to intensive exam prep, and there's pressure to join. What do you do?
options: A) Politely decline, explain your reason briefly, and suggest an alternative time to meet.
B) Agree to join but then bail last-minute without notice.
C) Go silent in the chat and don't respond either way.
D) Cave in and join anyway, then feel resentful about lost study time.

Set 4.text: You've been rejected from a summer research program you spent weeks preparing an application for. What's your next step?
options: A) Reapply to the exact same program next cycle without changing your approach.
B) Reach out to the program for feedback if possible, and look for a smaller-scale way to gain similar experience.
C) Assume the selection process was based on connections rather than merit.
D) Decide research isn't for you and rule it out as a future direction entirely.

Set 4.text: A classmate who recently moved from another city seems to be struggling to fit into established friend groups. What's your approach?
options: A) Feel bad for them but don't actually do anything differently.
B) Ask them directly why they're struggling to make friends.
C) Make a genuine effort to include them in plans without making it feel like charity.
D) Assume they'll figure it out on their own eventually.

Set 4.text: You and your best friend are applying to the same competitive college program, and it's starting to create quiet tension between you. How do you address it?
options: A) Downplay your own effort around them to avoid seeming like competition.
B) Secretly start keeping your prep strategies from them.
C) Avoid discussing the topic altogether and hope the tension fades on its own.
D) Name the tension directly and agree on how you both want to handle it, win or lose.

Set 5.text: You realise you feel a strange sense of relief whenever a class or activity gets cancelled, even ones you claim to enjoy. What might this mean?
options: A) Consider whether you're more overloaded than you've been admitting, and reassess your commitments.
B) Ignore the feeling since cancellations are rare anyway.
C) Feel guilty about the relief without exploring why you feel it.
D) Decide you're just lazy and leave it at that.

Set 5.text: A group project teammate publicly takes credit for an idea that was actually yours, in front of the teacher. What's your response in that moment?
options: A) Say nothing at all and let it go completely, even though it bothers you.
B) Let the moment pass, then calmly clarify your contribution with the teacher afterward, and address it with the teammate separately.
C) Stop contributing ideas to the group from that point on.
D) Interrupt loudly to correct them in front of everyone.

Set 5.text: You're preparing for a competitive exam and your rank in practice tests has plateaued for weeks despite consistent effort. What's your approach?
options: A) Assume you've hit your natural ceiling and can't improve further.
B) Increase study hours dramatically without changing your method.
C) Seek out a mentor or teacher to review your approach, since plateaus often mean it's time to change strategy, not effort.
D) Blame the test series for being poorly calibrated.

Set 5.text: A friend who's usually easygoing snaps at you unexpectedly over something minor. What's your first instinct?
options: A) Snap back immediately since their reaction feels unfair.
B) Avoid them for a while until the awkwardness passes.
C) Take it personally and assume they're upset with you specifically.
D) Consider that something else might be weighing on them, and ask calmly if everything's okay.

Set 5.text: Your parents want you to pursue a specific field, but you're increasingly certain you want something different. How do you approach the conversation?
options: A) Prepare your reasoning clearly, acknowledge their concerns, and have an honest, calm conversation about it.
B) Avoid the topic indefinitely and let them assume you're still on their preferred path.
C) Agree to their path outwardly while secretly planning to do your own thing later.
D) Announce your decision abruptly during an unrelated argument.

Set 6.text: You notice you only feel confident about your abilities right after getting a good grade, and completely doubt yourself after a bad one. What does this suggest?
options: A) Try to only focus on subjects where you consistently do well.
B) Recognise your self-esteem is overly dependent on external results, and work on building a steadier sense of your own ability.
C) Avoid checking grades as a way to sidestep the mood swings.
D) Decide your self-worth is just naturally tied to your grades and that's fine.

Set 6.text: During a heated class debate on a topic you care about, you feel your voice starting to shake with emotion. What do you do?
options: A) Push through anyway, letting the emotion take over the argument.
B) Raise your voice further to compensate for feeling emotional.
C) Take a breath, ground your point in facts, and let the passion come through without losing control of the argument.
D) Stop talking abruptly and let someone else take over.

Set 6.text: You didn't get selected as team captain despite putting in the most visible effort all season. What's your reaction?
options: A) Quietly resent the new captain without saying anything.
B) Decide effort clearly doesn't matter and stop trying as hard.
C) Assume the selected captain was just more popular, not more capable.
D) Ask the coach what qualities they were looking for, and consider how to build those for next time.

Set 6.text: A classmate you're not particularly close to shares that they're dealing with a family financial crisis. What's your response?
options: A) Listen without judgment, and avoid asking prying questions about details they haven't offered.
B) Feel awkward and quickly steer the conversation elsewhere.
C) Offer unsolicited advice about how their family should manage money.
D) Tell other friends so they can also be sensitive around the topic.

Set 6.text: You're mediating between two friends after a serious falling-out, and both are pressuring you to agree they were right. How do you handle it?
options: A) Pick the side of whoever seems more upset in the moment.
B) Stay neutral, validate both of their feelings, and encourage them to talk to each other directly.
C) Tell each of them privately that you agree with them, to keep both relationships intact.
D) Withdraw from the friendship entirely to avoid the pressure.

Set 7.text: You realise you've said 'I'm fine' to concerned friends multiple times this week when you clearly weren't. What's the pattern?
options: A) Assume no one actually wants to hear the real answer anyway.
B) Feel frustrated that people keep asking without doing anything differently.
C) Recognise you're minimising your own stress to avoid burdening others, and consider being more honest with at least one trusted person.
D) Decide it's easier to just keep saying that and move on.

Set 7.text: You get major feedback criticism on a college application essay you were proud of, right before the deadline. What's your immediate reaction?
options: A) Panic and rewrite the entire essay from scratch in a rush.
B) Dismiss the feedback entirely and submit the essay unchanged out of frustration.
C) Get defensive and argue with whoever gave the feedback.
D) Sit with the disappointment briefly, then objectively evaluate which points are worth addressing.

Set 7.text: A subject you need for your intended career path turns out to be much harder for you than expected. What's your approach?
options: A) Invest in extra support (tutoring, study groups) specifically for that subject, since it matters for your goal.
B) Blame the subject's difficulty on poor teaching rather than engaging with it.
C) Do the minimum to pass and hope it won't matter later.
D) Decide the career path isn't right for you based on this one subject.

Set 7.text: A friend seems to be pulling away from your friend group generally, not just from you specifically. What's your approach?
options: A) Assume they've simply outgrown the group and let it happen.
B) Reach out individually to check in, separate from the group dynamic, without pressuring them to explain.
C) Discuss it with the rest of the group before talking to them directly.
D) Take it personally and assume you did something to cause it.

Set 7.text: You're organising a farewell event and a teacher keeps overriding your team's decisions without much explanation. How do you handle it?
options: A) Complain to your team about the teacher without addressing it with them directly.
B) Push back publicly in front of the team to defend your original plan.
C) Request a brief conversation to understand their concerns and find a version both sides are comfortable with.
D) Comply silently with every change even when you disagree.

Set 8.text: You notice that you procrastinate most on tasks connected to subjects you secretly doubt you're good at. What's the real pattern?
options: A) Decide those subjects just aren't interesting to you.
B) Avoid the subject as much as possible going forward.
C) Push through with forced effort without addressing the underlying doubt.
D) Recognise the procrastination is likely fear of confirming self-doubt, and try starting with a small, low-stakes step.

Set 8.text: You're rejected from a leadership position you campaigned hard for, and the announcement happens in front of the whole class. What's your reaction in the moment?
options: A) Keep your composure publicly, and process the disappointment privately afterward.
B) Visibly show frustration or walk out of the room.
C) Force a big smile while feeling completely numb inside, avoiding the feeling entirely.
D) Make a sarcastic comment about the results to nearby classmates.

Set 8.text: Despite consistent practice, your public speaking still feels shaky compared to peers in your debate club. What's your approach?
options: A) Assume the club's standards are just unreasonably high.
B) Ask a mentor to record and review your speeches for specific, actionable feedback.
C) Decide public speaking isn't a skill you'll ever have and avoid it going forward.
D) Keep practicing the exact same way, hoping repetition alone will fix it.

Set 8.text: A friend didn't get into any of their top-choice colleges while others around them celebrate their own acceptances. What's your approach around them?
options: A) Tell them not to worry since 'everything happens for a reason'.
B) Avoid mentioning your own good news around them to spare their feelings, without checking how they'd actually prefer it.
C) Ask them directly how they'd like you to handle discussing college news around them right now.
D) Talk about your own acceptances at length since it's exciting news.

Set 8.text: You strongly disagree with a decision your student council makes, but you're outvoted. How do you proceed afterward?
options: A) Quietly stop participating in council activities without explanation.
B) Try to undermine the decision informally through side conversations.
C) Publicly criticise the decision to other students after the meeting.
D) Voice your disagreement clearly during the discussion, then support the group's decision once it's made.

Set 9.text: You realise your motivation to study spikes right before deadlines but is nearly absent otherwise. What's the honest read?
options: A) Recognise this pattern often leads to avoidable stress, and experiment with smaller intermediate deadlines for yourself.
B) Blame the workload for being unreasonably spaced out.
C) Decide you 'work best under pressure' and leave it at that permanently.
D) Assume there's nothing you can do to change how your motivation works.

Set 9.text: You receive a college rejection email while sitting in class and have to keep functioning normally for the rest of the day. What's your approach?
options: A) Distract yourself completely and refuse to think about it until much later.
B) Acknowledge the disappointment internally, compartmentalise for now, and process it properly once you have space and privacy.
C) Text multiple people immediately during class to vent about it.
D) Let the disappointment visibly affect your participation for the rest of the day.

Set 9.text: You've committed to an ambitious personal project (like a portfolio or research paper) and are now realising it's much bigger than you planned for. What's your move?
options: A) Abandon the project since it feels too overwhelming now.
B) Keep the scope the same and just work longer hours to compensate.
C) Scope it down to a realistic version you can actually complete well, rather than abandoning it entirely.
D) Blame yourself for poor initial planning and spiral into self-criticism.

Set 9.text: A friend is clearly avoiding a topic (like their exam results) that everyone else keeps casually bringing up. What's your approach?
options: A) Ask other friends what happened instead of asking them directly.
B) Bring it up directly since avoiding it seems unhealthy.
C) Assume it's not a big deal since they haven't mentioned it themselves.
D) Follow their lead and don't force the topic, while making it clear you're available if they want to talk.

Set 9.text: You and a friend group member have fundamentally different opinions on a sensitive current-affairs topic that comes up often. How do you manage the friendship?
options: A) Agree to disagree respectfully, and consciously keep the friendship bigger than this one topic.
B) Bring it up repeatedly, hoping to eventually change their mind.
C) Avoid the friendship altogether to sidestep the disagreement.
D) Pretend to agree with them just to keep things comfortable.

Set 10.text: Looking back at the year, you realise you've been defining your worth almost entirely by comparison to a specific high-achieving classmate. What's the takeaway?
options: A) Try to avoid that classmate altogether to stop the comparison.
B) Recognise the comparison has become more harmful than motivating, and consciously shift toward tracking your own progress instead.
C) Feel bad about the pattern without changing anything about it.
D) Decide the comparison is just useful motivation and keep doing it.

Set 10.text: You're in the final stretch before a major exam and a well-meaning relative keeps asking pressuring questions about your preparation. What's your response?
options: A) Avoid that relative completely for the rest of the exam period.
B) Snap at them out of accumulated stress.
C) Politely but firmly set a boundary about not discussing exam pressure right now, then redirect the conversation.
D) Answer every question anxiously in detail, feeding your own stress further.

Set 10.text: After a full year of consistent effort, your final results are only marginally better than last year's. What's your reflection?
options: A) Blame the grading or teachers for not recognising your effort.
B) Conclude that effort simply doesn't translate to results for you.
C) Decide to work exactly twice as hard next year without changing strategy.
D) Look honestly at what worked and what didn't, and use it to make next year's approach more effective, not just harder.

Set 10.text: A close friend is making a major life decision (like a gap year) that you privately think is a mistake. What's your approach?
options: A) Share your honest concerns once, clearly, then respect that it's ultimately their decision to make.
B) Tell them directly and repeatedly that they're making the wrong choice.
C) Say nothing at all, even though you have real concerns.
D) Talk to their parents about your concerns instead of them.

Set 10.text: As you and your close friends prepare to head to different colleges, tension is building around who's 'moving on' too fast or too slow emotionally. How do you handle it?
options: A) Match whichever friend seems most upset, to avoid seeming uncaring.
B) Name the shared anxiety about the transition openly, and suggest a way to stay connected that works for everyone.
C) Distance yourself early to make the eventual separation less painful.
D) Avoid the topic of the upcoming separation altogether to sidestep tension.

Set 1.text: You bomb a college presentation and notice your hands were shaking throughout. What's your read on this afterward?
options: A) Recognise the physical anxiety response, and identify specifically what preparation would help you feel steadier next time.
B) Blame the professor for making the room feel intimidating.
C) Decide you're just bad under pressure and start avoiding presentations.
D) Brush past it without reflecting on what happened.

Set 1.text: Your roommate keeps leaving shared spaces messy despite you mentioning it once already. What do you do?
options: A) Blow up over it the next time you find a mess.
B) Have a direct, calm conversation proposing a simple shared system (like a cleaning schedule).
C) Vent to other friends about it instead of addressing it with them.
D) Passive-aggressively leave their mess untouched to make a point.

Set 1.text: You don't get shortlisted for a competitive internship you prepared for over a month. What's your next step?
options: A) Decide you're not cut out for that industry and pivot away entirely.
B) Reapply to the same place next cycle without changing anything.
C) Reach out for feedback where possible, and apply to a few other opportunities with a refined approach.
D) Assume the process was rigged toward people with connections.

Set 1.text: A coursemate you barely know seems to be struggling significantly, missing classes and looking withdrawn. What's your approach?
options: A) Mention your concern to a mutual friend instead of reaching out yourself.
B) Assume it's not your place to say anything since you're not close.
C) Ask them directly and bluntly what's wrong with them.
D) Send a low-pressure message letting them know you've noticed and you're around if they need anything.

Set 1.text: You're assigned a group project with someone known for being difficult to work with. How do you approach the collaboration?
options: A) Set up an early conversation to agree on roles, communication style, and expectations upfront.
B) Complain to the professor preemptively about the group assignment.
C) Do the work independently and just merge it at the end with minimal contact.
D) Let them take the lead entirely to avoid any friction.

Set 2.text: You realise you've been more anxious than usual since starting to compare your post-graduation plans to your friends' plans. What's the underlying issue?
options: A) Decide your plans are objectively worse than everyone else's.
B) Recognise this is a comparison-driven anxiety, and refocus on whether your own plan actually fits your goals.
C) Avoid conversations about post-graduation plans altogether.
D) Change your plans impulsively to match what seems more impressive.

Set 2.text: A professor gives blunt, critical feedback on your thesis proposal in front of your cohort. What's your reaction in the moment?
options: A) Go quiet and mentally check out for the rest of the session.
B) Argue back defensively to justify your choices.
C) Accept the feedback calmly in the moment, and process any frustration privately before deciding what to revise.
D) Vent visibly to classmates immediately after the session ends.

Set 2.text: You've sent out dozens of job applications with almost no responses after weeks of searching. What's your approach?
options: A) Conclude that you're simply unemployable and stop applying.
B) Keep sending the exact same application to more places without changes.
C) Blame the job market entirely without examining your own approach.
D) Review your resume/approach for patterns, seek feedback from a career counsellor or mentor, and adjust your strategy.

Set 2.text: Your close friend gets a job offer at their dream company while you're still job hunting. What's your reaction?
options: A) Feel the sting honestly, and still show up as genuinely happy for them.
B) Downplay how good the offer actually is when discussing it with them.
C) Distance yourself from them to avoid feeling worse by comparison.
D) Avoid discussing your own job search around them out of discomfort.

Set 2.text: You and your roommate have different standards for guests staying over, and it's starting to create tension. How do you address it?
options: A) Complain about it to other friends instead of addressing it directly.
B) Propose a specific, clear agreement (like advance notice for overnight guests) in a calm conversation.
C) Say nothing and just quietly resent the situation.
D) Start inviting your own guests over more often out of spite.

Set 3.text: You notice you feel a specific dread every Sunday evening thinking about the coming week. What's your read on this?
options: A) Decide Sundays are just naturally a bad day and leave it at that.
B) Distract yourself from the feeling every Sunday without examining it.
C) Consider what specifically about the week ahead triggers the dread, and address that root cause directly.
D) Assume this is just what adult life feels like and accept it passively.

Set 3.text: A group chat for your friend circle turns into an argument about money owed from a trip, and it's getting heated. What's your role?
options: A) Stay silent in the chat but feel increasingly frustrated.
B) Add your own grievance to the pile since it's already tense anyway.
C) Leave the chat to avoid dealing with the conflict.
D) Suggest moving the money discussion to a calmer, separate conversation away from the group chat.

Set 3.text: You fail a core course and need to retake it, delaying your graduation timeline. What's your approach?
options: A) Accept the setback, understand exactly what went wrong, and build a concrete plan for the retake.
B) Retake it with the same study approach as before, hoping for a different outcome.
C) Spiral into feeling like your whole academic path is now ruined.
D) Blame the course structure entirely without reflecting on your own preparation.

Set 3.text: A coursemate confides they're struggling to afford tuition and might have to drop out. What's your response?
options: A) Offer to lend them money immediately without asking what kind of support they actually want.
B) Listen fully first, and ask what kind of help (if any) would actually be useful before offering anything specific.
C) Feel uncomfortable and change the subject.
D) Tell them things will work out without really engaging with the seriousness of it.

Set 3.text: You're leading a student club and a founding member resists any changes you propose, even reasonable ones. How do you handle it?
options: A) Avoid proposing further changes to prevent conflict.
B) Rally other members against them to override their objections.
C) Have a private conversation to understand their attachment to the old way, and find where you can incorporate their input.
D) Push through changes regardless of their objections since you're technically in charge.

Set 4.text: You realise that your mood at college has become almost entirely dependent on how your latest assignment grade came back. What's the takeaway?
options: A) Decide grades should determine your mood since that's what college is about.
B) Try to check grades less often without addressing the underlying reliance.
C) Feel bad about the pattern without changing anything.
D) Recognise this dependency is unsustainable, and work on building a sense of self-worth outside of grades.

Set 4.text: You're interviewing for a job and the interviewer asks an unexpectedly aggressive, pointed question about a gap in your resume. What's your response?
options: A) Pause briefly, then answer honestly and calmly with the context they're asking about.
B) Get visibly flustered and give a rambling, defensive answer.
C) Push back on the interviewer for asking an unfair question.
D) Deflect the question entirely without really answering it.

Set 4.text: Your startup idea/side-project gets rejected by every investor or incubator you pitch to. What's your next move?
options: A) Decide the idea (and you) simply aren't good enough and shelve it entirely.
B) Gather specific feedback across pitches to identify a common thread, and revise the pitch or the idea itself.
C) Conclude investors just don't understand your vision.
D) Pitch the exact same deck to more people without changes.

Set 4.text: A friend going through a breakup keeps bringing it up in every conversation, even weeks later. What's your approach?
options: A) Tell them directly to get over it and move on.
B) Start avoiding hanging out with them to escape the repetitive conversations.
C) Continue being patient and present, while gently checking if they've considered additional support if it's not improving.
D) Change the subject every time it comes up without acknowledging it.

Set 4.text: Your project partner in a capstone course consistently does lower-quality work than you, risking your shared grade. How do you handle it?
options: A) Report the imbalance to the professor without discussing it with your partner first.
B) Redo their sections yourself without telling them, to protect your grade.
C) Accept the lower quality and hope it doesn't affect your grade much.
D) Have a direct conversation about the quality gap, and agree on a review process before final submission.

Set 5.text: You've started dreading checking your email because of the anxiety around job application responses. What's the honest pattern here?
options: A) Recognise the anticipatory anxiety, and set specific times to check rather than checking constantly.
B) Avoid checking email altogether, even for unrelated important things.
C) Check obsessively anyway despite the anxiety it causes.
D) Decide checking email is just inherently stressful for everyone.

Set 5.text: A friend makes a joke at your expense in front of a group you're trying to make a good impression on. What's your reaction?
options: A) Go quiet and withdraw from the conversation for the rest of the evening.
B) Laugh it off in the moment if it's minor, and mention privately later if it actually bothered you.
C) Snap back with something equally cutting to save face.
D) Confront them loudly in front of the group.

Set 5.text: You take on a demanding part-time job alongside coursework and your grades start slipping as a result. What's your approach?
options: A) Push through exactly as-is, assuming things will sort themselves out.
B) Blame the coursework for being unreasonably demanding.
C) Honestly assess whether the current balance is sustainable, and adjust hours or study strategy accordingly.
D) Quit the job abruptly without a backup financial plan.

Set 5.text: A close friend seems to be making a financially risky decision (like an expensive impulsive purchase) during a hard time. What's your approach?
options: A) Lecture them repeatedly about financial responsibility.
B) Talk to mutual friends about your concern instead of them.
C) Say nothing since it's their money and their choice.
D) Express your concern honestly once, in a caring way, then respect their autonomy either way.

Set 5.text: You need to tell your close college friend group that you're moving to a different city after graduation, which will change the dynamic. How do you approach it?
options: A) Share the news openly and early, and talk through how you all want to stay connected.
B) Downplay the significance of the move to avoid an emotional conversation.
C) Avoid bringing it up until it becomes unavoidable.
D) Let someone else break the news for you.

Set 6.text: You notice you get defensive almost instantly whenever anyone offers feedback on your work, even constructive feedback. What's the underlying pattern?
options: A) Avoid sharing work with others to sidestep the discomfort of feedback.
B) Recognise the defensiveness as a pattern worth examining, and practice pausing before responding to feedback.
C) Accept the feedback outwardly but dismiss it internally without reflection.
D) Decide your work is generally strong and people are just being overly critical.

Set 6.text: You're rejected from your top-choice grad school program while a peer with a similar profile gets in. What's your reaction?
options: A) Publicly express frustration about the unfairness of admissions.
B) Assume the process was unfair or based on favoritism.
C) Feel the disappointment fully, then look for a constructive next step (reapplication, alternative program, feedback).
D) Avoid the peer and any conversation about the topic entirely.

Set 6.text: Your thesis advisor suggests scrapping months of research direction for a different approach close to the deadline. What's your response?
options: A) Refuse outright since you've already invested so much time.
B) Agree immediately without pushing back, even if you have real concerns.
C) Feel like all your prior work was wasted and lose motivation to continue.
D) Take time to genuinely evaluate the suggestion, then decide on a path forward with a clear rationale.

Set 6.text: A coursemate is visibly anxious before a group presentation and asks to go last, which shifts more prep pressure onto others. What's your response?
options: A) Accommodate their request where possible, while checking in with the group about fair distribution of prep work.
B) Push back and insist they take their originally assigned slot.
C) Agree without discussing the impact on the rest of the group.
D) Complain to the group about them being difficult.

Set 6.text: You and your long-distance college friend are drifting apart as your lives diverge post-graduation. How do you handle it?
options: A) Cut contact abruptly rather than deal with the gradual distance.
B) Have an honest conversation about the changing dynamic and agree on a realistic way to stay in touch.
C) Overcompensate with excessive messages to try to force the old closeness back.
D) Let the friendship fade naturally without addressing it.

Set 7.text: You realise you feel disproportionately anxious about small mistakes at your internship compared to how your supervisor actually reacts to them. What's your read?
options: A) Assume your supervisor is secretly more upset than they're letting on.
B) Avoid taking on tasks where mistakes are possible.
C) Notice the gap between your internal reaction and the actual stakes, and work on calibrating your response to feedback.
D) Decide you're just naturally more anxious than other interns.

Set 7.text: You get a lowball job offer after a long, exhausting interview process and feel a surge of frustration. What's your next move?
options: A) Accept it immediately just to end the exhausting process, despite your frustration.
B) Reject it immediately and angrily without further discussion.
C) Vent about the company publicly on social media.
D) Take time to process the frustration, then respond professionally to negotiate or decline.

Set 7.text: You've been passed over for a promotion at your part-time job that you were hoping for. What's your approach?
options: A) Ask your manager directly what would strengthen your case for next time.
B) Decide the workplace doesn't value you and start disengaging.
C) Keep doing exactly the same work, hoping it'll be noticed eventually.
D) Assume it was purely political and stop putting in extra effort.

Set 7.text: A new intern on your team is clearly overwhelmed and making small mistakes trying to keep up. What's your approach?
